audit_trail.notes: All three reviewers operated under a shared epistemic constraint: the review bundle contained only the abstract, embedded author footnotes, and the 86-item reference list. The main article text and Supplemental Material (containing beta functions, slow-roll analysis, perturbation spectrum derivations, and reheating calculations) were inaccessible due to a paywall/bundle-extraction limitation, even though the article is published under a CC BY 4.0 open-access license. Gemini's cycle 1 scores of 0/0/2 for clarity/technical_soundness/overall were rejected by the other two reviewers as conflating bundle limitations with paper quality; Gemini revised upward in cycle 2. The ghost/unitarity prescription concern, RG scale ambiguity, large-N fine-tuning, and Gauss-Bonnet omission survived both cycles as the four most robust objections. The paper's PRL publication (March 2026) was treated by Claude as a meaningful prior toward technical competence, while Codex weighted it less heavily given the unresolved ghost sector question.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper proposes a new theory of cosmic inflation rooted in "quantum quadratic gravity," a version of Einstein's gravity extended with extra curvature terms that is mathematically well-behaved at arbitrarily high energies. Quantum corrections naturally drive the universe through an inflationary phase, and the theory makes a concrete, testable prediction: future gravitational-wave detectors should see a minimum signal above a specific threshold. Reviewers found the idea timely and the publication venue (Physical Review Letters) credible, but the full mathematical derivations were inaccessible behind a paywall, so key technical claims could not be independently verified. The most important unresolved question is how the theory handles a known instability (the "ghost" mode in the higher-curvature sector) and whether the main quantitative prediction is robust to acknowledged approximations.
human_spotchecks: Which specific ghost/unitarity prescription (fakeon, Lee-Wick, PT-symmetric, causal Donoghue-Menezes, or other) is used for the spin-2 mode of the Weyl-squared sector, and is it applied consistently through the background equations of motion, scalar perturbation spectrum, tensor perturbation spectrum, and reheating calculation?

Is the r ≥ 0.01 lower bound robust to alternative choices of the RG scale identification at the end of inflation, where the authors acknowledge that different curvature-invariant choices become inequivalent? A sensitivity analysis or explicit prescription-independence argument is needed.

What is the explicit numerical range of N (number of matter fields) required to achieve viable ns and r under ACT DR6 and SPT-3G constraints, and is this range compatible with any plausible UV-complete matter sector? Is N ∼ O(10), O(100), or larger?

Does the reheating mechanism produce a concrete reheat temperature estimate, and is it consistent with the BBN lower bound of T_reh ≳ 4 MeV? This is a necessary consistency check for the UV-completion-of-the-Big-Bang claim.

Where exactly (in terms of the slow-roll parameter ε or number of e-folds before the end of inflation) does the 1-loop approximation lose quantitative control, and do the observable CMB scales exit the Hubble radius within the regime of loop control?
public_summary: Liu, Quintin, and Afshordi (Waterloo/Perimeter/McGill, published in Phys. Rev. Lett. 136, 111501, March 2026) present a quantum quadratic gravity (QQG) inflationary scenario motivated by recent CMB data from ACT DR6 and SPT-3G that have disfavored the previously canonical Starobinsky R² model. The paper argues that QQG is asymptotically free in the ultraviolet—a property supported by several decades of prior literature—and that 1-loop renormalization-group running dynamically drives the theory into a slow-roll inflationary phase toward the infrared. Phenomenological compatibility with the spectral index requires contributions from a large number N of matter fields in the beta functions; the authors acknowledge but do not resolve whether this constitutes a fine-tuning. As inflation ends, the theory is claimed to approach strong coupling, from which general relativity must emerge as an effective field theory before standard reheating. The paper's key falsifiable prediction is a minimum tensor-to-scalar ratio r ≥ 0.01, positioned to be tested by next-generation experiments (CMB-S4, LiteBIRD). The current BICEP/Keck upper bound r < 0.036 is consistent with this prediction. Review was conducted under a significant epistemic constraint: all three reviewers had access only to the abstract, scattered author footnotes, and the 86-item reference list; the main article text and Supplemental Material (which contain the beta functions, slow-roll analysis, perturbation spectrum derivation, and reheating calculation) are behind a paywall and could not be inspected. The review board's conclusions are therefore necessarily limited to what can be inferred from the abstract and the authors' own disclosures in footnotes.
reviewer_disagreements: Verdict divergence: Claude (both cycles) recommended 'promising'; Codex (both cycles) recommended 'contested'; Gemini recommended 'weak_evidence' in cycle 1 and revised to 'contested' in cycle 2 after recognizing that the inaccessible main text is a bundle/paywall artifact rather than a flaw in the paper. The 'promising' vs. 'contested' split between Claude and Codex persisted through cycle 2, with Claude citing the PRL publication as a meaningful peer-review prior and Codex maintaining that unresolved ghost prescription and large-N dependence keep the verdict contested.

Severity of the 'GR emergence asserted without derivation' objection: Codex rated this as major and verdict-changing (confidence 0.69–0.72); Claude rated it as moderate, arguing that GR emergence as an EFT from UV-complete gravity at strong coupling is a standard assumption in this research program and that the derivation is described as present in the Supplemental Material.

Severity of Weyl tensor perturbative contributions to the tensor spectrum: Codex rated this as moderate and verdict-changing (confidence 0.66); Claude rated it as minor and not verdict-changing, noting that the Weyl tensor vanishes exactly on FRW backgrounds and that Clunan & Sasaki 2010 (ref 34) is a published result supporting the suppression claim for tensor perturbations.

Gemini cycle 1 assigned clarity = 0 and technical_soundness = 0 with an overall score of 2, treating the absence of the main text from the review bundle as a fatal technical objection to the paper. Claude and Codex both identified this as a methodological error (conflating reviewer access limitations with paper quality) rather than evidence of paper weakness. Gemini accepted this critique and revised scores upward in cycle 2.

strongest_objections.objection: The Weyl-squared term in quadratic gravity propagates a spin-2 ghost (Ostrogradski instability). Multiple competing prescriptions exist for handling it (fakeon/Lee-Wick, PT-symmetric, Donoghue-Menezes causal prescription). Which prescription is adopted and whether it is applied consistently through background evolution, scalar perturbations, tensor perturbations, and reheating cannot be determined from the available text. This is not a peripheral issue: the physical Hilbert space of the theory and the validity of the ns and r predictions depend on this choice.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract and reference list (Donoghue-Menezes refs 17–24, Holdom-Ren refs, Bender-Mannheim ref 16)

strongest_objections.objection: The paper's central quantitative prediction—r ≥ 0.01—is derived from the condition that the theory avoids strong coupling at the end of inflation. The authors themselves acknowledge in a footnote that different choices of curvature invariant as RG scale 'are generically equivalent to the Ricci scalar' during slow roll 'but will start to differ as we approach the end of inflation.' The r bound is derived in exactly this regime of acknowledged prescription ambiguity, and no sensitivity analysis is presented.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Footnote on RG scale choice; Abstract

strongest_objections.objection: Phenomenological viability of the spectral index and tensor-to-scalar ratio requires a large number N of matter fields contributing to the beta functions. The authors explicitly 'remain agnostic about whether or not a very large N represents a fine-tuning problem' and speculate that these fields 'would probably end up being confined to the UV.' No specific value of N is stated in the available text, no UV-complete matter sector is proposed, and no bound from known physics constrains N from above or below.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract; footnote beginning 'One may question how large can reasonably be'

strongest_objections.objection: The running of the Gauss-Bonnet coupling κ_GB is explicitly ignored. The authors acknowledge in a footnote that this 'may affect cosmological evolution—as in f(R,G) theories.' In four dimensions the Gauss-Bonnet term is topological at the classical level but its running coupling is not, and it can enter the effective action at loop level and modify background dynamics.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Footnote beginning 'Here, we ignore the impact of the running of the coupling to Gauss-Bonnet's invariant'

strongest_objections.objection: The mechanism by which GR emerges from the strong-coupling regime and the universe reheats into a standard radiation-dominated era is asserted in the abstract as necessary but is deferred entirely to Supplemental Material, which is inaccessible. No matching conditions, reheating temperature estimate, or BBN consistency check are visible in the reviewed material.

The paper invokes 1-loop RG running throughout the inflationary trajectory but simultaneously concludes that the theory approaches strong coupling at the end of inflation. The regime in which the r ≥ 0.01 bound is derived is therefore one where higher-loop or non-perturbative corrections may be important, yet the validity of the 1-loop approximation in this regime is not established in the available text.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract; footnote on end of inflation and strong coupling

strongest_supported_claims: UV asymptotic freedom of quantum quadratic gravity is well-established in the prior literature, supported independently by Fradkin-Tseytlin (1982), Avramidi-Barvinsky (1985), Codello-Percacci (2006), Niedermaier (2009/2010), Holdom-Ren (2016), and others cited in the reference list; the paper's reliance on this property is on solid footing.

The claim that Starobinsky inflation is disfavored by ACT DR6 (Louis et al. 2025) and SPT-3G (Camphuis et al. arXiv:2506.20707) reflects an established community result; the observational motivation for seeking an alternative is real and timely.

The predicted r ≥ 0.01 is a concrete, currently observationally consistent, and in-principle falsifiable lower bound: the BICEP/Keck limit (r < 0.036 at 95% CL) is compatible, and CMB-S4 and LiteBIRD are expected to probe this range.

The paper passed peer review at Physical Review Letters (received November 2025, accepted February 2026, published March 2026), providing an independent prior that domain experts found the core framework technically coherent.
The broad program of RG-improved inflation in quadratic gravity is coherent with the existing literature on which the paper builds, including the authors' own prior Phys. Rev. D 111, 044031 (2025).
technical_takeaway: The paper argues that quantum quadratic gravity (QQG), which is UV asymptotically free, dynamically generates slow-roll inflation via 1-loop renormalization-group (RG) running, becoming viable under post-Starobinsky observational constraints (ACT DR6, SPT-3G) when a large number N of matter fields contribute to the beta functions. The central falsifiable prediction is a minimum tensor-to-scalar ratio r ≥ 0.01, derived from the condition that the theory must exit the strong-coupling regime before reheating. Three reviewers over two cycles identified four persistent technical concerns that survived cross-review: (1) the unitarity/ghost prescription for the spin-2 mode in the Weyl² sector is not verifiable from the available text and is foundational to the entire perturbation calculation; (2) the r ≥ 0.01 bound is derived in the end-of-inflation regime where the authors themselves acknowledge that RG scale identification becomes prescription-dependent; (3) the large-N matter sector required for a viable spectral index is explicitly acknowledged as potentially unnatural, with no UV-complete realization offered; and (4) running of the Gauss-Bonnet coupling is self-admittedly omitted despite acknowledged possible impact on the cosmological evolution. All evaluations were conducted under a shared epistemic constraint: only the abstract, embedded footnotes, and reference list were accessible; main text and Supplemental Material were paywalled.
